title: The Smoke-Country Eastward

features.name: distant-fire
features.desc-on-touch: Smoke rises from somewhere you cannot reach. The fire has burned here three times before, each time the country recovers differently. The archive records each regrowth as a separate text.

features.name: heat-shimmer
features.desc-on-touch: The air bends and re-bends. Objects in the distance appear and disappear. You cannot tell if the country is there or if the smoke is creating it from the heat.

features.name: burnt-ground-signature
features.desc-on-touch: The earth here holds three different signatures of burning, layered under fresh growth. The archive is learning to read fire as a form of comparison—each burn tells what the last one forgot.

features.name: smoke-record
features.desc-on-touch: A post, blackened at the base. On it, tally marks in soot—counting the seasons since the last fire, or perhaps counting fires yet to come. The numbers have begun to repeat.

features.name: eastern-horizon
features.desc-on-touch: The smoke thickens toward the horizon. Through it, the faint outline of a town—one you have never seen, one that only exists in the smoke-haze. Or one that exists every time a fire burns.

inscription: the country burns in cycles and forgets its own pattern each time the ash clears

file.name: Smoke_Cycles_And_Regrowth_Eastern_Fires.txt
file.note: Field recording: audio of wind moving through burnt scrub, voices of people who returned after fire, the sound of growth returning. Each replay sounds different.

reverie.title: REVERIE — smoke country, watching fire mark the land again
narr: You stand with her on the eastern plain, watching
smoke bend into shapes the fire will not hold. She says
the country remembers each burn differently, and the
fire knows this. That's why it keeps returning—to teach
the land new ways to grow. The embers fall like slow questions.
She touches your arm. Neither of you knows the answer.

exits.label: back across the open country, away from smoke
into the town that only exists in smoke-haze
toward higher country where fire burns differently
down to older ash, where the soil still remembers being coal
